meta.focus: The pivotal transition to plain-form speech: forming plain negative and past-negative, connecting clauses with が/けど (but) and から/ので (because), plain commands, and the なんです explanatory form.

sections.title: From Polite to Real Japanese — Plain Forms Unlocked
sections.summary: So far you have used polite verb endings: ます, ました, ません. These are correct and respectful — but they are not the only forms Japanese uses. Real conversation, subordinate clauses, connectors, and most writing all rely on plain forms. This lesson unlocks the full plain-form system.
sections.whyItMatters: Plain forms are not 'casual slang' — they are the grammatical foundation of Japanese. Relative clauses (the food I ate), reported speech (she said she went), conditionals, and reason clauses all require plain form, even in polite contexts. Mastering plain forms is the single most important step toward fluency after the polite basics.
youWillLearn: Plain negative: 食べない, 飲まない, しない, こない — how to say 'don't do' in plain speech
Plain past negative: 食べなかった — how to say 'didn't do' in plain speech
が and けど — formal and casual ways to say 'but' and link contrasting clauses
から and ので — two ways to say 'because', each with different nuance and formality
Plain commands (～ろ/～え) and plain prohibition (～な) — with strong register warnings
なんです/のです — the explanatory form that softens statements and invites understanding

sections.title: The Four Plain Forms — Complete Verb Chart
sections.description: Every Japanese verb has four plain forms. You already know the plain present (dictionary form) and plain past (た-form from G7). This lesson adds the plain negative (ない) and plain past negative (なかった).
notes: Polite equivalents: 食べます / 食べました / 食べません / 食べませんでした. Plain forms are shorter and used in casual speech and subordinate clauses.
来る (くる) is fully irregular: present くる, past きた, negative こない, past-neg こなかった.
ある has a special irregular plain negative: ない (not あらない). ある → ない is the most important exception to memorize.
Plain forms are required: (1) in casual speech with friends/family, (2) inside subordinate clauses, (3) before connectors like けど, から, ので, and conditional forms.

pattern.label: IRREGULAR
pattern.text: する→しない / くる→こない / ある→ない

sections.explanation:
Plain negative formation follows three rules depending on verb type:

**RU-verbs (ichidan):** Drop る, add ない.
食べる → 食べない. 来る (ichidan) → actually irregular: こない.

**U-verbs (godan):** Shift the final sound to the あ-column of its row, then add ない.
飲む → 飲ま+ない = 飲まない (む→ま is the あ-column of む's row)
行く → 行か+ない = 行かない (く→か)

⚠️ **Special exception — う → わ + ない:**
Godan verbs ending in vowel+う shift to わ (NOT to あ).
買う → 買わない (not 買あない). 言う → 言わない.

**Irregulars (memorize):**
する → しない
くる → こない
ある → ない ⚠ The plain negative of ある is simply ない — not あらない.
notes: あ-column shifts for godan: く→か, ぐ→が, す→さ, つ→た, ぬ→な, ぶ→ば, む→ま, る→ら. Each is the 'a' vowel of that consonant's row.
The う→わ exception: 買う→買わない, 言う→言わない. Any godan verb ending in う after a consonant follows this rule.
ある→ない is the most common exception. ある means 'there is'; ない means 'there is not / does not exist'.
Plain past negative: take the plain negative, remove い, add かった. 食べない → 食べなかった. This follows い-adjective past conjugation rules.

examples.breakdown: 飲む (godan む): む → ま (あ-column) + ない = 飲まない.
買う (godan う): う → わ (special exception) + ない = 買わない. Never 買あない.

examples.en: There is no curry at the convenience store.
examples.breakdown: ある → ない (fully irregular). The negative of ある (there is) is just ない (there isn't) — not あらない.

sections.title: Polite Forms vs. Plain Forms — When to Use Each

items.label: Polite (ます/ません/ませんでした)
points: Used with strangers, teachers, in shops, at school — any non-intimate context
Ends in ます (present), ました (past), ません (negative), ませんでした (past neg)
Always appropriate — the safe default for learners
Cannot be used inside subordinate clauses (relative clauses, conditionals, etc.)

parts.text: → food は 食べません

example.en: 食べません (polite) — I don't eat.

items.label: Plain (dictionary / ない / なかった)
points: Used with close friends and family, in writing, in subordinate clauses
Ends in dictionary form (present), た (past), ない (negative), なかった (past neg)
The grammatical base — all other forms derive from it
Required in subordinate clauses even in otherwise polite sentences

example.en: 食べない (plain) / 食べなかった (plain past neg)

sections.explanation:
が and けど both mean 'but' when connecting two clauses with a contrasting or softening relationship. They differ in register:

**が (formal/neutral):** Attaches directly to the end of a clause in polite or plain form. Common in formal speech, writing, and polite conversation.
行きたいですが、お金がありません。(I want to go, but I have no money.)

**けど / けれど (casual/softer):** The informal equivalent of が. Attaches to plain form. Common in conversation with friends and family. けれど is slightly more formal than けど.
行きたいけど、お金がない。(I want to go but I have no money.) — casual

⚠️ **Important: do not confuse clause-linking が with subject-marker が (G2).** They use the same particle but have completely different functions.
- Subject marker: カレーが おいしい (curry is delicious) — が marks the subject
- Clause connector: 行きたいですが、… (I want to go, but…) — が links two clauses mid-sentence
notes: が as clause connector attaches mid-sentence. It does NOT start a new sentence. 行きたいですが、… is one sentence.
Both が and けど can soften a statement by leaving the second clause implicit: 行きたいですが… (I want to go, but [implied: I can't]). Trailing が/けど is very common in polite requests.
けど is grammatically correct in all casual contexts — not slang. けれど is its more literary/formal written form.
Register guide: 行きたいですが (formal) > 行きたいですけど (semi-formal) > 行きたいけど (casual) > 行きたいけどさ (very casual).

examples.en: Curry is delicious, but it is expensive.
examples.breakdown: Polite form + が: おいしいです + が + 高いです. Formal register — appropriate with teachers, in shops, etc.

examples.en: Curry is delicious, but it's expensive. (casual)
examples.breakdown: Plain form + けど: おいしい (plain) + けど + 高い (plain). Casual register — appropriate with friends.

pattern.label: REASON CLAUSE
pattern.text: おいしいから / おいしいので

sections.meaning: から / ので — 'because / since' (reason connector)
sections.explanation:
Both から and ので mean 'because' and connect a reason clause to a main clause. In Japanese, the reason clause comes FIRST.

**から (direct/personal):** Attaches to plain or polite form. Common in both casual and polite speech. Presents the reason as the speaker's personal interpretation.
カレーが おいしいから、よく 食べます。(Because curry is delicious, I often eat it.)
カレーが おいしいですから、よく 食べます。(polite form + から)

**ので (soft/objective):** Attaches to plain form before ので. For nouns and な-adjectives, insert な before ので. More formal, objective, and indirect than から. Preferred in writing and polite speech when the reason is a shared/natural fact.
カレーが おいしいので、よく 食べます。(same meaning, softer tone)

⚠️ **Note on から:** You already know から as a starting-point particle (G3): 東京から = 'from Tokyo'. This is a different usage — the same particle word, but working as a clause connector meaning 'because'. Context always makes the meaning clear.
notes: Word order: [REASON] + から/ので + [RESULT]. Opposite of English! 'Because A, B' = AだからB / AなのでB.
Plain form + から: most natural in casual speech. Polite form + から: appropriate in polite speech.
ので + na-adjective / noun: noun + な + ので (e.g. 先生なので = because [he] is a teacher).
ので is softer and implies the reason is obvious or shared — slightly less assertive than から.
から as connector vs から as 'from': 駅から = 'from the station' (location); 高いから = 'because it is expensive' (reason). Both are grammatically correct uses of から.

examples.en: Because curry is delicious, I often eat it.
examples.breakdown: Plain adj (おいしい) + から + main clause. Reason comes first. から is direct and natural.

examples.en: Since there is a convenience store, it is fine.
examples.breakdown: Polite form (あります) + ので. Softer and more formal than から. Used when the reason is a natural/given fact.

sections.title: から vs ので — Same Meaning, Different Nuance

items.label: から — Direct / Personal
points: More direct and assertive — the speaker takes ownership of the reason
Natural in both casual and polite speech
Can attach to plain form (casual) or polite form
Common when explaining your own decisions or feelings

example.en: Because it's expensive, I won't buy it.

items.label: ので — Soft / Objective
points: Softer and more indirect — the reason feels like a natural, shared fact
Preferred in formal writing, business contexts, and polite requests
Attaches to plain form (or noun/na-adj + な + ので)
Gives the impression of stating an obvious or common-sense reason

example.en: Since it's expensive, I won't buy it. (softer)

sections.meaning: Plain command (～ろ/～え) and plain prohibition (～な) — blunt register
sections.explanation:
Japanese has a plain imperative form for strong, direct commands. These are grammatically correct but extremely blunt and potentially rude.

**Plain commands (～ろ/～え):**
- RU-verbs (ichidan): drop る, add ろ → 食べる → 食べろ ('Eat!')
- U-verbs (godan): shift to え-column → 行く → 行け ('Go!'), 飲む → 飲め ('Drink!')
- する → しろ. くる → こい (unique irregular form).

**Plain prohibition (dictionary form + な):**
- Any verb + な = blunt prohibition: 食べるな ('Don't eat!'), 行くな ('Don't go!')
- ⚠️ This な is different from the sentence-final な expressing mild prohibition; this one is very forceful.

⚠️ **REGISTER WARNING — These forms sound harsh or rude in most situations.** Use them only:
- In emergencies ('Stop!' 止まれ！)
- In anime/manga or between very close friends joking
- When imitating a drill sergeant or authority figure
- In set expressions (e.g., 気をつけろ = 'Be careful!')

**Polite equivalents:** Use ～てください (G7) for requests, and ～ないでください (G7) for prohibitions in everyday speech.
notes: え-column shifts for U-verb commands: く→け, ぐ→げ, す→せ, つ→て, ぬ→ね, ぶ→べ, む→め, る→れ. Remember: the 'e' vowel of each row.
来い (こい) is the irregular command form of 来る. It is the only command form that does not follow the て or え pattern.
Plain prohibition: DICTIONARY FORM + な. 食べるな (don't eat). Compare: 食べるな (blunt) vs 食べないでください (polite request).
Do NOT confuse command 食べろ with volitional 食べよう ('let's eat') — they look similar but mean very different things.

examples.en: Eat quickly! (blunt — e.g., shouted in an anime scene)
examples.breakdown: 食べる (ichidan): drop る → 食べ + ろ = 食べろ. Extremely direct — only use in appropriate contexts.
走る (dictionary form) + な = prohibition. Much stronger than 走らないでください.

pattern.label: PLAIN FORM
pattern.text: おいしい / 行く / 先生だ

pattern.label: + のです
pattern.text: おいしいんです / 行くんです / 先生なんです

sections.meaning: のです / なんです — the explanatory / emphasis form
sections.explanation:
のです (spoken/casual: んです) adds an explanatory or emphatic nuance. It signals that the speaker is providing a reason, explanation, or context — or asking for one.

**Formation:**
- Verb (plain form) + のです → 行くのです / 行くんです (I'm going, you see)
- い-adjective (plain form) + のです → おいしいんです (It's delicious — that's why!)
- Noun / な-adjective: insert な before のです → 先生なんです (The thing is, he's a teacher)

**Contracted form:** のです → んです (common in speech). のです is more formal/written.

**Uses:**
1. **Explaining a reason:** どうして 外食を しませんか。— お金が ないんです。(Why won't you eat out? — The thing is, I have no money.)
2. **Seeking an explanation (question):** どうして 行かないんですか。(Why is it that you're not going?)
3. **Emphasizing a fact:** 本当においしいんです！(It really is delicious, I'm telling you!)
4. **Softening a request:** 行きたいんですが、いいですか。(The thing is, I'd like to go — is that okay?)
notes: な before のです is only required for nouns and な-adjectives. 先生 (noun) → 先生なんです. い-adjectives and verbs attach directly.
Negative form: ～ないんです or ～くないんです. 行かないんです = 'The thing is, I'm not going'.
んですが (trailing) is one of the most natural ways to make a request or introduce a topic politely: 外食を したいんですが、いっしょに 行きませんか。
だ (casual copula, G1) changes to な before の/ん: 学生だ → 学生なんだ (casual) / 学生なんです (polite).

examples.en: The thing is, I don't have money.
examples.breakdown: ない (plain negative of ある) + んです. This explains WHY something is happening or not happening.

parts.text: いっしょに 行きませんか。

examples.en: The thing is, I want to eat out — shall we go together?
examples.breakdown: たいんですが trails off, inviting the listener to respond. A very natural way to make a polite suggestion or request.

sections.title: かいわ 1: 外食しませんか — Formal Register (が/から)
sections.context: りきぞ and やまかわ are at school planning lunch. They use polite forms throughout, with が (but) and から (because) as natural connectors.

lines.jp: 今日は いっしょに 外食を しませんか。
lines.en: Today, shall we eat out together?

lines.jp: 行きたいですが、お金が ありません。
lines.en: I want to go, but I have no money.

lines.jp: コンビニへ 行きませんか。カレーも ありますよ。
lines.en: Shall we go to the convenience store? There is curry there too.

lines.jp: カレーは おいしいですから、コンビニが いいですね。
lines.en: Since curry is delicious, the convenience store is a good idea.

lines.jp: そうですね。いっしょに 行きましょう。

sections.title: かいわ 2: 外食しない？ — Casual Register (けど/plain forms)
sections.context: The same situation, now between close friends. They use plain verb forms and けど (but) instead of formal が. Notice how the register shifts while the topic stays the same.

lines.en: Today — going out to eat, right? (casual invitation)

lines.jp: 行きたいですけど、お金が ない。
lines.en: I want to go, but I have no money.

lines.jp: コンビニに 行かない？
lines.en: How about going to the convenience store?

lines.jp: コンビニの カレーは おいしいよ。
lines.en: The convenience store curry is delicious, you know.

lines.jp: おいしいから、行きましょう。

sections.title: Plain Negative Practice — Dictionary Form → ない
sections.instructions: Convert each verb to its plain negative form.

items.hint: RU-verb: drop る, add ない → 食べ + ない
U-verb む: shift to あ-column (む→ま), add ない → 飲ま + ない
U-verb く: shift to あ-column (く→か), add ない → 行か + ない
U-verb う (special): う → わ + ない. Never 買あない!
する is irregular: する → しない (memorize)
くる is irregular: くる → こない (memorize)
ある is fully irregular: ある → ない (not あらない!)

sections.title: Polite to Plain — Register Conversion
sections.instructions: Transform each polite form into its plain equivalent, or combine two clauses using the connector shown.

items.given: カレーは おいしいです。高いです。
items.answer: カレーは おいしいですが、高いです。
choices: カレーは おいしいですが、高いです。
カレーは おいしいから、高いです。
カレーは おいしいので、高いです。
カレーは おいしいけど、高い。

sections.title: Which Connector Fits? — が / けど / から / ので

items.explanation: Polite form + が = formal 'but'. The sentence ends in ですが to contrast two polite clauses. けど also means 'but' but is casual. から/ので mean 'because', not 'but'.

items.before: カレーが おいしい
items.explanation: Plain adjective (おいしい) + から = 'because it's delicious'. The reason clause comes FIRST in Japanese. から is direct and natural in personal contexts. ので is also 'because' but more formal/objective.

items.before: コンビニへ 行きたい
items.explanation: Plain form (行きたい) + けど = casual 'but'. The plain form ending signals casual register — use けど, not formal が. が requires polite forms. から/ので = because.

items.before: コーヒーが すきです
items.explanation: Polite form + から = polite 'because'. The reason (liking coffee) comes before the result (often drinking it). Japanese reason-result order is the reverse of English.

items.before: 明日 テストが あります
items.after: 、今日は 外食を しません。
items.explanation: Polite form + ので = formal/objective 'because'. ので is preferred when the reason is a natural, shared-understanding fact (having a test is an objective situation). から is also correct but sounds more personal.

items.before: レストランの カレーは おいしいけど
items.explanation: けど already contains the 'but' connector. Nothing additional is needed — just a comma before the contrasting clause. The sentence ends in plain form (高い) confirming casual register throughout.

sections.title: G9 れんしゅう — Plain Forms & Connectors
sections.instructions: Choose the correct answer for each question.

items.q: What is the plain negative of 飲む?
items.explanation: 飲む is a godan (U) verb ending in む. あ-column shift: む → ま + ない = 飲まない. Polite negative is 飲みません (ます-stem + ません), not the same as plain negative.

items.q: What is the plain negative of ある?
items.explanation: ある is fully irregular. Its plain negative is ない (there isn't / does not exist). Never あらない — that form does not exist. ありません is the polite negative (not plain).

items.q: Which sentence correctly uses が as 'but'?
choices: 行きたいですが、お金が ありません。
行きたいますが、お金が ありません。
行きたいですが お金が ありません
行きたいが、ですがお金が ありません。
items.answer: 行きたいですが、お金が ありません。
items.explanation: が attaches directly to the end of the first clause: 行きたいですが. There must be a comma before the second clause. The first clause uses polite form (たいです) — this is the formal register for が.

items.q: In Japanese, where does the reason clause go when using から?
choices: Before the main clause (reason + から + result)
After the main clause (result + から + reason)
から always begins the sentence
から always ends the sentence
items.answer: Before the main clause (reason + から + result)
items.explanation: Japanese word order for reason expressions is the REVERSE of English. In Japanese: [REASON] + から + [RESULT]. Example: カレーが おいしいから、よく 食べます。('Because curry is delicious, I often eat it.') The reason clause comes before the main clause.

items.q: Which is the blunt plain command form of 行く (Go!)?
items.explanation: 行く (godan く): え-column shift: く → け = 行け. This is the plain imperative — blunt and potentially rude. 行こう = 'let's go' (volitional, not a command). 行かない = plain negative. 行きろ does not exist.

items.q: Which sentence correctly uses なんです to explain a reason?
choices: お金が ないんです。
お金が ないをんです。
お金がんです ない。
お金が ないのがです。
items.answer: お金が ないんです。
items.explanation: Plain form (ない) + んです = explanatory/emphasis. お金が ないんです = 'The thing is, I have no money.' The plain negative of ある (ない) connects directly to んです without any additional particles.
